draft.introduction: In Germania 9, Tacitus describes the religious practices of the Germanic peoples. They worship in sacred groves rather than temples, and their chief deities correspond roughly to Mercury, Hercules, and Mars—though Tacitus emphasizes that they resist confining the gods to human form.

translation.text: Among the gods, Mercury is the one they chiefly worship, and on certain days they deem it right to sacrifice to him even human victims. Hercules and Mars they appease with more lawful offerings. They do not consider it consistent with the grandeur of celestial beings to confine the gods within walls, or to liken them to the form of any human countenance. They consecrate woods and groves and apply the names of deities to that hidden presence which they see only with the eye of reverence.
translation.original_text: Deorum maxime Mercurium colunt. Ceterum nec cohibere parietibus deos neque in ullam humani oris speciem adsimulare ex magnitudine caelestium arbitrantur; lucos ac nemora consecrant.

translator_notes.note: Tacitus’s interpretatio Romana—identifying Germanic gods with Roman equivalents—obscures the native theology. Mercury likely corresponds to Wodan (Odin), Hercules to Donar (Thor), and Mars to Tiwaz (Tyr). The aniconic worship described here reflects a genuinely archaic religious practice.
